notes.anchor: Philotimus was a partner in his property
notes.note: After Milo's conviction for the murder of Clodius and his exile to Massilia (52 BC), his estate was sold up; Cicero had arranged for Terentia's freedman Philotimus to buy a share, ostensibly to protect Milo's interests rather than let strangers strip the property.

notes.anchor: what he had wished secured for Fausta might be made firm
notes.note: Fausta, daughter of the dictator Sulla and wife of Milo, was to have a settlement guaranteed out of the estate; Cicero's management of the sale aimed to keep that provision intact.

notes.anchor: the very many slaves he has with him
notes.note: Milo had taken a large household of slaves into exile at Massilia; a hostile purchaser of the estate might have claimed and seized them, which Cicero's arrangement was meant to prevent.
